summary: DeVault praises Alpine Linux for being boring and reliable, contrasting it with distributions like Red Hat that frequently generate controversy. He describes using Alpine across all his computing contexts—workstations, servers, phones, development boards—and finding it consistently uneventful. The post implicitly argues that the best infrastructure is invisible, and that Alpine's grassroots, stakeholder-driven community avoids the drama that comes with commercial interests. He positions Alpine's absence from headlines as its greatest strength.
key_points: Alpine Linux is boring and reliable, which is exactly what a Linux distribution should be
Grassroots, stakeholder-driven maintenance without centralized commercial interests avoids the controversies plaguing Red Hat, Ubuntu, and Debian
Alpine's community is organized so that users can easily contribute to maintaining the distribution for their own use-cases
The absence of entities trying to monetize Alpine means the community remains aligned on its fundamental purpose
money_quotes: Over the years I have used it, very little of note has happened.
It has been a boring experience. The system is simply reliable, and the upgrades go over without issue every other quarter.
Generally speaking, Alpine does not make the news.
I simply keep running "apk upgrade" every now and then and life goes on uninterrupted.
The Alpine community is a grassroots set of stakeholders who diligently concern themselves with the business of maintaining a good Linux distribution.
There are no commercial entities which are trying to monetize it.
The community is largely in unanimous agreement about the fundamental purpose of Alpine and the work of the community is focused on maintaining the project such that this purpose is upheld.
This is a good trait for a Linux distribution to have.
key_insight: The best Linux distribution is one that never makes the news, because drama-free reliability is the product of aligned community incentives and the absence of commercial monetization pressure.
